author: @stevehowie2819
text: Dr. Murray-Smith, I really enjoy your progress and ideology on your battery development. I'm planning to build a solar/electric catamaran to do the "Great Loop" , an 8000 mile journey up the east coast and through the central river system to Florida and back. My design challenge thus far has been hindered by battery logistics. Am I correct in assuming that the technology you're working on would be mouldable? I would like to essentially turn each hull into giant batteries, assuming weight wouldn't be too large. I'd appreciate you're thoughts.

author: @Khwartz
text:
9:44 - Employees who are Ready To Work For Free Very Because Of The Job Itself, and not for the salary, is VERY what an entreprise needs the most, but it is the reason also they should be Financially Rewarded For Their Positive Efforts :)

Very Great You've made this video, Mates, that was Very interesting indeed :)

So, if You don't aim to manufacture, how You can help a manufacture in France to make batteries megacapacitors that can do Better Than 400 mWh/g?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree and she doesn't work for nothing mate - she was joking. But Mandy has a great deal of commitment as does everybody here at FWG. If somebody wants to manufacture for France we would help them set up - they would need to buy a licence of course

replies.author: @Khwartz
replies.text:
I'm not surprised She was Joking. Nevertheless, I was speaking of The State Of Mind the Employee will actually work with ;-)

~°~

Indeed, it is Exactly what we have already discussed here: we pay You a Licence and You help us to manufacture them here locally :) and this, with also the terms I've just proposed to You in my previous comment:

- "and reserve a part of our Financial Benefit to Spread The Technology Throughout The World and For The Cheapest Cost As Possible, including possible Visits In Poor Countries To Show Them How To Do By Themselves."

I'm waiting any answer by mail how we can organise (have You possibly on phone or else) and make this Real, as soon I will have produced myself a Green 18650 Cell that can beat the present dirt cells used in the actual market.

Best Regards, Dearest Mate :)

replies.author: @Khwartz

author: @Leviathan15s
text:
The problem I see with selling IP is that you could invent the best cheapest green graphene battery and have the IP right bought by a future competitor of that battery, like say a lithium battery company or any battery company for that matter, only for them to hold on to that IP to make sure nobody else develops so they don't have any competition for their current products. I really hope something like this doesn't happen but I wish you the best of luck, I would really like to see commercial graphene batteries in the future. 

Cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It doesn't matter what you do mate - there are always problems but given the number of folks working on this i think you are expounding a pretty unlikely scenario

replies.author: @loungelizard836
replies.text: I wouldn't worry so much about other battery manufacturers, they would all love to make a better product as it would mean more money.  Oil companies, though... that's a concern, as happened with the Ovonics NiMH patent (stolen by GM and resold to Texaco (now Chevron).  "ChevronTexaco also maintained veto power over any sale or licensing of NiMH technology.[13] In addition, ChevronTexaco maintained the right to seize all of Cobasys' intellectual property rights in the event that ECD Ovonics did not fulfill its contractual obligations.[13] "

author: @Razyre
text: Great to learn more about how things work at the lab Robert, I mentioned about being in the area a little while ago and would love to come down and see the lab, wouldn't want to get under your feet though. What would be best for you?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is no best at the moment i am afraid mate - just come when you want and i will make time for you

replies.author: @Razyre
replies.text: I get you, hopefully I'll be able to come down in the next few weeks when I'm back in town. Really excited to see what you're doing there.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome

author: @skaltura
text:
Nice to hear you guys have made a good progress! :)

Graphite powder, what process do you use to make graphene out of it?
Cosolvent, or make it first solid and electrolysis?

I've got 25kg bag (~3.5€ per kg) of graphite powder to test with, making it work with electrolytic exfoliation method hasn't yet been working... I guess time to go buy a shop press and try to turn it into solid like they do industrially and attempt again (i tried different bonding methods... either too fragile or lost conductivity)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and we use a few different processes but i have done videos on them all

replies.author: @skaltura
replies.text:
Yes, the cosolvent (water acetone) is an obvious method ... But i really like the electrolysis method's speed and bulk nature ... So need to first turn the powder into a solid form for it to work as electrode.

I guess i have to decide whether to buy shop press, or keep buying small chunks of ebay or something :/

I use A LOT of graphene, doing composites. 100grams don't last long ... Infact, quite easily used on a single laminate

replies.author: @skaltura
replies.text:
likely is, but peek is stupid stupid expensive. You might want to look into ABS Graphene or even PLA Graphene mixture on common use where part cost is an factor.
PEEK when cost is not an factor :)

author: @danielfeyerabend904

Dr. Smith and company,
Great work!  You certainly are busy.  Your couch and coffee idea sessions must be exciting also.  Here's a thought for the mix: foam-Crete with graphene and the 3d cement applying printers.  Inexpensive and durable homes for us common folk!

author: @DanielSMatthews
text:
Some guys have all the fun!

That was very interesting. I guess what you are doing is the future of work for many people too, the creative application of science toward the generation of industrially applicable intellectual property. When AI and robots are doing all the piece work for humans we will be free to be inventive.

Your lab seems to have the sort of configuration I'd like to set up for my children so that they can make the most of the STEM education I am personally giving them, so they can be the masters of their own destiny rather than salary slaves.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: spot on mate - nice comment and i agree with what you are saying here

author: @MarkSeve

author: @stephanegagne403
text: just a question for you are there any known carbons that could Electrostaticly cling to a conductive material like aluminum or copper instead of having to use a binder mixture to make it stick ..for the making of capacitors

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no idea mate - but a search of google scholar should help

replies.author: @stephanegagne403
replies.text: Why i had asked is because I can't find a carbon that does or any information on it ... but strangely enough I managed to create such a carbon just the other day still testing it out wanted to see if you might have any other insight ...Thank you so much though my friend

replies.author: @stephanegagne403
replies.text: It clings to glass and aluminum and copper I forgot to add it acts like a magnet but isn't it has a very strong Electrostatic charge to it

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it sounds like you have some awesome stuff there mate - you should do some videos - of the properties maybe and your investigations - i'd love to see

replies.author: @stephanegagne403
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith ...I should do a video and defenitly will Robert would love to share it ...I promise I will just want a little time first to discover for myself first what I actually made ..all I know for now is yes it has some surprising properties ..I tested it out on a small aluminum capacitor sit it on top of a tin can and did exceptionally very good it also plated the top of the can aluminum ...I didn't use any other carbons for the test ...I uniquely used the carbon I made as the cathode carbon and also the same for the anode carbon....when  place it on my kitchen table or walk around with it you can feel either a pulling or strong repelling force in your hands when holding the stuff...also I believe in the Same process I believe I found the way to mass produce graphene ...I'm very excited right now ...still want to play around with it more and test more of it out before doing a video but I am anxious to do one .. I'm still trying to figure out exactly what I have created ... tried googling tried everything else then said to myself I have to ask Robert if anything he should know ....that's what I told my wife...lol

author: @NeoShameMan
text:
Still no progress on solar graphene?
I have always been interested in changing plain carbon into graphene, if this is something we can do in the kitchen it could lead to increase personal self sustainability (my end goal, off the grid lifetyle) and autonomous factory.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you know - i was thinking about this as i put the video up - I was thinking that despite doing a video showing how much work it was and how much work we had done and how we work 14-16 hours a day constantly to get done the stuff we do some one some where would ignore that and ask about why wasn't this done yet - or why wasn't that done - turns out you are the first to do something like that - well done mate lol

author: @Crina-LudmilaCristeaAuthor
text: Hi! My partner and I watch your videos and just as we were watching the newest one, I was just thinking about the sound. We love your videos and it would be great if the sound is even better. I bought a microphone recently to use in interviews with people. It's very nice and can be used both with phones and cameras. It works with most models. It's not wireless but for the price and it's usefulness it's worth using. We would love to send you one if you could provide us with an adress we can send it at. ☺

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya Lily - i really do appreciate the offer but we use a zoom h2n microphne it's a really good bit of kit - the problem with the sound isn't really the quality of microphone - it's more to do with the acoustics of where we are and the ineptitude of the people using it. I am afraid this is a window into the lab so we won't be hanging acoustic baffles anytime soon but i really do appreciate the offer

replies.author: @Crina-LudmilaCristeaAuthor
replies.text: The sound in this video is very good. Just the begining bit sounds as if you didn't have a microphone, the rest is ok (except the acoustics issue but that is what it is and not a real issue). I was just thinking in terms of when you are recording alone. Anyway, looking forward to your videos.  ☺🤗🤗

author: @loungelizard836
text:
Very interesting things going on Robert, thanks for showing us all the shop! 
Your building has a lot of "echo".   I'm sure the echo doesn't affect everyone the same, but for many people it makes communication difficult.  It certainly makes your videos hard to listen to.  Please consider hanging some sort of sound-absorbent materials about the place.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry  mate - but, it's a lab not a recording studio - so there is no chance of hanging a lot of sound absorbing materials around

replies.author: @loungelizard836
replies.text: Well, that's certainly your call to make... it is YOUR lab!  If you should decide to do so it can be as simple as gluing carpet pieces to the wall.  Many options, personally I find that sound-dampened areas are less distracting and more pleasant.  Usually a more pleasant work environment is also more productive (slave labor camps not included)!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it is mate - I don't really know much about you but if you have a lab go right ahead and soundproof it as that would be your decision - i find the more pleasant environment is made from the people who work in it

replies.text: cheers mate

author: @algirdaslazauskas2733
text: You guys have a nice place there ;) I know by heart that it is very useful to put chemicals by-hand, unfortunately   it does not work for the health and safety requirements, e.g. in your video +16 gone wrong, please consult in this case as it could vary in EU.p.s.  I appreciate your capacitor progress.Please note, that your ideas are still patented by huge companies....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - and you can't patent an idea mate only the implementation of an idea

replies.author: @algirdaslazauskas2733
replies.text: Actually, the idea and testing of it is the main kernel what anyone could think about. However, this could be easily bypassed using common alteration techniques ;)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - I don't want to be rude here - but you think I don't know this?

author: @arthurschroeder3003
text: Thanks bud, amazing the way things are coming together. Good Luck.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @servant74
text:
Thanks for the update.  It is actually helpful in understanding what is going on.

I know you had printing presses at one time, I assume those were sold or otherwise disposed of (pretty large for your lab!).

Being a wannabe entrepreneur, I was wondering what your general cash flow (sources and expenditure) categories are.  (No, I don't want to see financials, but an understanding about where money comes from and where it is going -- in broad general strokes). ... If that is to proprietary, just forget it. ... Thanks again for keeping us involved in your efforts!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the big machines you talked about are in the background of the new production space mate - as for finances - I ain't saying mate

replies.author: @servant74
replies.text: Thanks friend!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Sorry mate, I changed my mind you are clearly being  good guy and i was being a bit of a dick - sorry mate - i do get tired - There's a number of places - we write books, do the odd job for folks, donations are a large part of it and we will sell the occasional product - quite a lot of it comes from our savings

replies.author: @servant74
replies.text:
No problem Robert.  I understand getting short especially if something might seem a bit intrusive, and if I get really tired.

Thank you for the kind reply.  That is as much as I was hopeing to get (no detail, nothing intrusive).  With your group being pretty much of an 'idea mill', I was expecting some significant part to be from licensing IP to companies that want to take various products to market. ... But that is just my musings, with nothing real to base it on.

Thanks again. ... Take care

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: we will probably get to something like that mate - JVs or IP licensing or even straight IP sales

author: @stephaneislistening6103
text:
Hi Rob, may I ask you what camera you use to film your videos ? Cheers, Stephane
PS: Thanks for the office tour !

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries mate and a canon EOS700D camera and zoom h2n microphone

replies.author: @stephaneislistening6103
replies.text: Rob, may I ask if you would accept a donation project being done, so we can all of us fund a mike for you and the team ? I could fire this on gofundme.com now.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I appreciate the thought mate but we use a zoom h2n mike  - it's a top of the line mike

author: @jouleml
text: Inspirational!...i live in Tanzania and have no clue what am I doing watching all of your videos in a country where no one seems to have heard about graphene yet! Two things made my day today - this video and a telephone conversation with perhaps one of earliest local chemistry professor who promised me to spend his day looking for a local supplier of graphite conductive paint...its 1700 hours now and still anxiously waiting for his call!!! Wish me luck please. lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lots of good luck mate lol

replies.author: @new_carbon_energy
replies.text: Hi Joules. I am from Africa too. We have amazing technology and resources in Africa. We make graphene! You can send me a mail at mail.antonj@gmail.com.

replies.author: @new_carbon_energy
replies.text: Many of the minarals used worldwide come from Africa. I visited a company in South Africa that do 3D printing with Titanium

author: @johnnydoe6325

author: @sedevacantist1
text: There is a lot of talk about sodium batteries.  Your opinion about sodium batteries would be interesting to a lot of people, thanks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't like to give opinions mate - I really don't know all that much and my ideas are pretty much as good as the next persons - i just do what i think is right but that doesn't mean  I think what others are doing is inferior - it's just different and there is a whole range of uses for a whole range of battery types

u have good energy.. maybe get some kids in so they can learn like an apprentice

replies.author: @skaltura
replies.text: Try the electrolysis method (0.5molar sulfate), i had great success with that, made a few hundred grams with cheap lab power supply and tiny jewelers sonicator, few coffee filters and random sulfates (i tried many of the common ones, even FeSo4 works!)
